coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensing of doxorubicin in unprocessed whole blood, cell lysate, and human plasma samples using thin film of poly-arginine modified glassy carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Soleymani, Jafar
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Eskandani, Morteza
Khoubnasabjafari, Maryam
Shadjou, Nasrin
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: A thin film of poly-arginine fabricated on glassy carbon electrode by one step electrodeposition method is applied for detection of doxorubicin hydrochloride in whole blood, cell lysate, and untreated-plasma samples. Cyclic voltammetry results indicated that the doxorubicin is oxidized via two electrons and two protons at physiological pH (pH=7.4) using poly-arginine thin film modified glassy carbon. More importantly, electrostatic repulsion takes place between the prepared polymer film-modified electrode and selected drug resulting in the signal amplification on oxidation of doxorubicin and lowering its over potential and thereby selective detection of doxorubicin in real samples. The apparent electron transfer rate constant and transfer coefficient were determined by cyclic voltammetry and were approximately 10.1s−1 and 0.82, respectively. Also, using differential-pulse voltammetric technique for sensitive detection of doxorubicin in whole blood and plasma samples, the lower limit of quantification was 69nM and 103nM, respectively. Also, application of this amino acid based biocompatible polymeric electrode was tested to the determination of doxorubicin in unprocessed whole blood and the results show that this sensor could be applied in online and real time monitoring of this anti-cancer drug in real samples which is important for clinical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxorubicin
Electrochemistry
Biological fluids
Poly-amino acid
Whole blood
Cell lysate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electropolymerization of l-arginine (ARG) on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE)
Characterization of PARG morphology using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM)
Determination in whole blood and plasma samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms for the electrochemical polymerization of 2.5mMARG on a GCE at the scan rate 100mV/s.
FEG-SEM image of GCE (A) and PARG-GCE (B).

A) CVs of GCE (a) and PARG-GCE (b) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) solution+25.86μM DOX. B) CVs of a PARG-GCE in PBS (without DOX) solution with increasing of the scan rate from inner to outer: 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700, 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 and 1000mV/s. C) CVs of a PARG-GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) solution+25.86μM DOX with increasing of the scan rate from inner to outer: 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700, 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 and 1000mV/s.
A) CVs of PARG-GCE in different pH of 0.12M PBS. (B and C) Influence of pH on the oxidation peak current and potential of PARG-GCE in 0.12M PBS (pH=7.4), respectively.
A) Variations of oxidation peak currents versus scan rate; B) Variations of oxidation peak currents versus square root of scan rate.

A) Dependence of the neperian logarithm of peak currents versus Ln ν for the oxidation of DOX at PARG-GCE. B) Variations of oxidation peak potential versus neperian logarithm of neperian logarithm of peak currents. C) Dependence of the oxidation peak potential versus neperian logarithm of scan rate. D) Dependence of anodic peak current (Ip) on potential scan rate (v).

A) DPVs of PARG-GCE in different concentrations of DOX in whole blood B) Calibration curve of DOX in whole blood. C) DPVs of PARG-GCE in different concentrations of DOX in plasma. D) Calibration curve of DOX in plasma.

A) The DPVs of PARG-GCE in the presence of 20μM DOX with different incubation time (0 (s-6), 1 (s-5), 2 (s-4), 12 (s-3), 24 (s-2), and 48 (s-1) hour); B) Dependency of peak currents of PARG-GCE versus incubation time of A549 cell with 20μM DOX.
Proposed mechanism of the preparation of PARG on the GCE.
Possible oxidation mechanism of DOX on PARG-GCE.
Analytical figures of merit of the DPV electrochemical method for determination of DOX using modified PARG-GCE electrode.
Analytical parameters for detection of DOX in real samples at different methods.

row.entry: Thiol base sol-gel (TBSol-Gel) modified gold electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Inter-day precision, accuracy and recoveries of the method for assay of DOX.
Intra-day precision, accuracy and recoveries for determination of DOX.
Details of the stability of the proposed method in whole blood and plasma samples.
Maximum tolerable concentration of interfering species on 1ppm of DOX in whole blood matrices.
Maximum tolerable concentration of interfering species on 1ppm of DOX in whole plasma matrices.
Tested whole blood and whole serum samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensing of doxorubicin in unprocessed whole blood, cell lysate, and human plasma samples using thin film of poly-arginine modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Center, and Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Center, and Faculty of Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A thin film of poly-arginine fabricated on glassy carbon electrode by one step electrodeposition method is applied for detection of doxorubicin hydrochloride in whole blood, cell lysate, and untreated-plasma samples. Cyclic voltammetry results indicated that the doxorubicin is oxidized via two electrons and two protons at physiological pH (pH=7.4) using poly-arginine thin film modified glassy carbon. More importantly, electrostatic repulsion takes place between the prepared polymer film-modified electrode and selected drug resulting in the signal amplification on oxidation of doxorubicin and lowering its over potential and thereby selective detection of doxorubicin in real samples. The apparent electron transfer rate constant and transfer coefficient were determined by cyclic voltammetry and were approximately 10.1s−1 and 0.82, respectively. Also, using differential-pulse voltammetric technique for sensitive detection of doxorubicin in whole blood and plasma samples, the lower limit of quantification was 69nM and 103nM, respectively. Also, application of this amino acid based biocompatible polymeric electrode was tested to the determination of doxorubicin in unprocessed whole blood and the results show that this sensor could be applied in online and real time monitoring of this anti-cancer drug in real samples which is important for clinical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX) is a clinically important anti-cancer agent which is extensively used in treatment of a broad variety of cancers. However, long-term clinical use of DOX is limited due to its cytotoxic effects on neoplastic cells through the accumulation of reactive oxygen produced by scavenger enzyme activities [1,2]. Therefore, monitoring of the level of this drug in biological fluids of patients during their therapy period is vital. A number of analytical methods such as high performance liquid chromatography [3–5], fluorescence spectroscopy [6,7], electrochemical sensors [8,9] and capillary electrophoresis [10–12] have been developed for determination of DOX in biological samples.

But each method has its drawbacks as well such as time consuming, laborious, expensive and low sensitivity. The possibility to selectively, analyze biological samples directly without fractionally pre-treatment, inexpensive equipment, simple manipulation and simple model system for calibration are the advantages of electrochemical methods [13–21]. Also, several electrochemical methods have been reported as simple and inexpensive methods for DOX determination in biological fluids [22–26]. These methods present satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity. Electroanalytical methods are appropriate alternative to the above mentioned methods, because of simple sample pretreatment procedures. Based on this fact that DOX contains electroactive groups, some electroanalytical methods based on the use of various working electrodes have established for analysis of DOX [22–26]. Hasanzadeh and Shadjou [27] and Soleymani et al. [28] reviewed the reported electrochemical and spectroscopic sensing and biosensing methods in whole blood media, where the reported methods along with their advantages and disadvantages were discussed in depth.

The modification of polymeric species by adsorption or coating on to the surfaces of electrode gives extensive flexibility as polymers containing a range of functional groups which can achieve extremely high surface coverage using thick multilayer coatings [29]. This property helps attach relatively some of the compound to the polymer matrix-coated electrodes and subsequently mediate the oxidation of electroactive analytes. Among the different methods for preparing polymeric-modified electrode, electropolymerization has appeared as a versatile and efficient approach because of its advantages in terms of strong adherence to electrode surface and chemical stability of the film analysis, high sensitivity, selectivity and homogeneity in electrochemical deposition, reduced costs and etc. [30–32]. The electropolymerization of organic molecules with suitable functional groups, (NH2, COOH OH, SH etc.) has exhibited to be a very convenient means for preparing functionalized and electroactive polymers at electrodes, because the procedure can be conveniently controlled by adjusting the electrochemical parameters. Accordingly, the charge transport, permeation, and thickness characteristics of the modified electrodes by polymer films can be enhanced. In addition, the electropolymerization of some organic molecules allows the production of new electrochemical sensors [33–35].

The NH2 and COOH groups of amino acids play a key role in the electro-polymerization process on the electrode surface [36]. Owing to the excellent electrocatalytic properties of amino acids, different types of poly amino acids (polypeptide) have been prepared by chemical and electrochemical methods for electrochemical sensor applications [37]. Among different monomers, bio-inspired research has found that arginine is able to undergo an oxidative polymerization reaction in marine condition and form strong adhesion on the substrate on which it is grown. In contrast to other well established techniques, such as mono-layer self-assembly, surface modification by poly-arginine (PARG) is carried out in an in-situ process. The PARG coating on the surface of substrates is typically thin and offers highly robust structure at a wide range of pH. Intrigued by the dormant potential of PARG, thus far it has been implemented as an electrochemical sensing platform for a myriad of substances. Therefore, PARG has been used for the modification of electrodes and applied for electrochemical determination, owing to its versatility and ease of preparation. However, only a few reports of the application of PARG for the development of modified electrodes have been recognized. Past studies have shown that the arginine exhibits high biocompatibility, high adsorption ability and the biological activity of redox proteins [38,39].

In this work, the first attempt to prepare ultra-thin film of PARG as electrochemical sensor via a facile electropolymerization process for selective and sensitive recognition of DOX in some real samples is made. An electropolymerized film of l-arginine was prepared on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The polymer film has high concentration of negatively charged functional groups (COO−). Therefore, electrostatic repulsion takes place between the prepared polymer film-modified electrode and the oxidized form of DOX resulting in the electrocatalytic oxidation of DOX by lowering its over potential and thereby selective detection of DOX in real samples. Under the optimal conditions, the PARG based sensor was successfully used for determining DOX in whole blood, cell lysate and plasma samples. Therefore, PARG modified GCE can be considered as a voltammetric sensor for the analysis of DOX in clinical analysis. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the determination of DOX based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on poly amino acid films. In addition this work is the first statement on determination of DOX in whole blood and cell lysate at physiological pH (pH=7.4) using a bio-polymeric sensor.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and were used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water. DOX was purchased from Exir Nano Sina Company (Tehran, Iran), its chromatographic purity was 98.5%.Double-distilled water was used for preparation of the solutions. Solutions were prepared from analytical reagent grade chemicals without further purifications using double distilled water. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.0.02M) was prepared form KH2PO4 and K2HPO4. The stock solution of DOX (1.7×10−4 M) was prepared by dissolving an accurate amount of bulk drug in an appropriate volume of 0.12M PBS, pH=7.4 (which was used also as supporting electrolyte), and then stored in the dark place at 4°C. Additional dilute solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. Also the other stock solutions were prepared the same as DOX solution by dissolving an accurate amount equal to molecular weight of each one in an appropriate volume of 1000mL deionized water and then all stored in the dark place at 4°C.

Human plasma and blood samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). Aliquots of plasma were transferred into micro-tubes and frozen at −20°C until analysis. Also, whole blood samples were kept in citrated tubes and stored at 4°C. Human plasma samples were thawed at room temperature daily and were vortexed to ensure homogeneity. After thawing the samples gently, 0.5mL of an aliquot volume of this sample was spiked with DOX. Approximately, 0.5mL of plasma/whole blood were taken and added into supporting electrolytes to reach a total volume of 5mL.

A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cell lines were grown in RPMI1640 medium with 10% FBS and 100U/ml penicillin and 100μg/ml streptomycin antibiotics in a humidified incubator with an atmosphere of 95% air and 5% CO2 at 37°C. To evaluate the uptake of doxorubicin by A549 cell lines, the cells were seeded in 6 well plates at the density of 4×105 cells/well and incubated for 24h. After then, the cells were treated with doxorubicin (IC50 concentration: 3.2μM) dissolved in RPMI and incubated for 2, 4, 6, 8, 12 and 24h at 37°C. Finally, the metabolic extraction was carried out based on methanol/chloroform method. Briefly, methanol (600μl) added to the cell suspension, vortexed, and incubated on ice for 15min, followed by addition of 600μl chloroform. The tubes were vortexed and centrifuged at 13,000rpm for 10min at 15°C. The two phases were transferred to a fresh tube carefully avoiding the interface. Chilled acetonitrile (ACN: 600μl) was added to each tube, vortexed, and incubated at −80°C for 2h. The tubes were centrifuged at 13,000rpm for 10min at 4°C. The supernatant was transferred to fresh tubes and dried under vacuum. The two tubes for each sample were combined by re-suspending in 150μl 50% ACN/water, followed by electrochemical analysis.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) powered by an electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using NOVA 1.11 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10mV and the equilibrium time was 5s. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was GCE (d=2mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran) and PARG-GCE. For DPV measurements, a pulse width of 25mV, a pulse time of 50ms, and a scan rate of 50mV/s were employed. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes was evaluated with a MIRA3 FEG-SEM (SEM, Hitachi Ltd., Czech).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of l-arginine (ARG) on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE)
ce:section.ce:para: Before to the electrode modification, the GCE with 2mm diameter were polished with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina (Buehler, USA) slurries, and then ultrasonically cleaned by water, ethanol, and water for 3min, respectively. The electrode was allowed to dry in air. CV was used to form the preparation thin film of PARG on the surface of GCE. Fig. 1 shows the continuous CVs of electrochemical polymerization of l-arginine on the surface of GCE. PARG film-modified GCE was fabricated by electrochemical polymerization of l-ARG at the GCE by CV method in 0.12M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) containing 2.5mM arginine at pH7.4. Electropolymerization was performed between the potential of −1.5 to 2V vs. Ag/AgCl at the scan rate of 100mV/s in 10cycles. During the polymerization process, indiscernible peaks started to appear after the 2nd cycle. A first anodic (1.52V vs. Ag/AgCl) and cathodic peak (−0.52V vs. Ag/AgCl) were observed due to the formation of PARG at the surface of GCE. The peak current increased with increase in the number of CV scans indicating that an electro-conductive polymer film was formed on the electrode surface. Finally, the oxidation peak unchanged after complete formation of PARG film. It has been confirmed that monomer containing NH2canbe electropolymerized on the surface of electrode to form a conducting film via covalent bond (CN) between electrode and NH2. L-ARG has two NH2 and two NH groups and that can easily be oxidized to produce NH2 Covalent bond (CN) is then formed by the free radical reaction of NH2 with GCE surface with excellent stability. Therefore, the covalent bond (CN) might be formed between carbon electrode and the α-amino group of arginine. It is important to point out that, during the high potential scan rate, the α-amino free radical will easily form and the polymerization of the poly amino acid film could be formed on the surface of GCE. The reaction mechanism may be described as Scheme 1 .

After 10cycles of CV sweeps, the electropolymerization procedure was finished and the polymer thin film electrode was washed with distilled water to remove the physically adsorbed materials. Then the film electrode was transferred to an electrochemical cell and CV sweeps were carried out to obtain electrochemical steady state. In order to confirm the formation of PARG on GCE, the CV sweep was carried out in 0.12M PBS (pH=7.4) in the range of −1 to +1V at 100mV/s. A broad CVs compared to blank was obtained which confirms the deposition of polymer film on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PARG morphology using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the surface morphology of the PARG film formed on the GCE surface, it was examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy. Fig. 2 shows the SEM images obtained for GCE, PARG-GCE, respectively. The GCE surface has a smooth morphology (Fig. 2A). Fig. 2B shows that considerable amounts of PARG with an average size of 50nm were formed on the surface of GCE. The FE-SEM for PARG-GCE shows regular modification of GC surface by PARG, which can be helpful in the providing an efficient interface for the following combination with analytes (DOX in this case). Finally, these results confirmed that the GCE was coated by PARG film, leading to the change in the surface activity of the electrode. Therefore, a biocompatible polymer (PARG) can be fabricated on the surface of glassy carbon electrode using electropolymerization.

In the present study, the electrochemical oxidation of DOX at the GCE and modified GCEs was investigated by voltammetric method. Cyclic voltammograms obtained in the presence of 25.86μM DOX (PBS, pH7.4) at a potential sweep rate of 100mV/s, at the bare GCE (curve a) and PARG-GCE (curve b) in the presence of DOX (Fig. 3 ). CVs results show that, using PARG-GCE and GCE one pair redox peaks appear. The comparison of recorded CVs using PARG-GCE and GCE indicate a new anodic peak at −0.70V vs. Ag/AgCl on the surface of PARG-GCE which obviously shows the important role of biopolymer film in the electrooxidation of DOX. On the other word, these results were ascribed to the satisfactory electroactivity of PARG which was due to the excellent characteristics of the biopolymer such as good electrical conductivity, high surface area and more electroactive interaction sites that can facilitate the electron transfer and charge exchange between the DOX and the modified electrode surface. Therefore, as expected for a modifier, PARG caused a decrease in the oxidation potential and increase in sensitivity of the prepared sensor.

This may be due to electrostatic repulsion/attraction of DOX on to the PARG film formed on the surface of GCE. After the formation of electropolymerized film of PARG-GCE on the electrode surface (Scheme 2 ), hydrogen bond forms between the hydrogen in hydroxyl-phenol on the benzene ring of DOX and the nitrogen in the system of PARG-GCE (Scheme 2). The hydrogen bond activates the hydroxyl-phenol on the benzene ring of DOX leading to weakening of the bond energy between hydrogen and oxygen. The bond of benzene ring and oxygen of DOX is prone to rupture, and as the oxidation process accelerates it leads to the enhancement of current of DOX at PARG-GCE.

The more active functional groups on the surface of the PARG can easily form hydrogen bonds with the amine groups of DOX, which weaken the NR bond energies; the electrons would be transferred through OH⋯NH. Therefore, signal enhancement lead to enhancement of the anodic peak for DOX oxidation.

The supporting electrolyte solution and pH value of this solution affect the peak currents and peak potentials of DOX. In order to understand the influence of supporting electrolyte on the linear sweep voltammetric behavior of DOX, three kinds of supporting electrolyte solutions (phosphate, acetate and citrate buffers) were used. The highest anodic peak current of DOX was observed in phosphate buffer solution which was chosen as working media.

The influence of the solution pH (phosphate buffer) on the electro-oxidation of DOX (25.86μM DOX) was investigated in the pH range of 3–11 (Fig. 4A). It was observed that the anodic peak current (I pa) reached its maximum value at pH=7.4 (Fig. 4B). Therefore, pH7.4 was chosen as the best pH condition for the following electrochemical measurements which is the physiological pH value. In this study, it was found that the oxidation peak potential of DOX shifted to less negative potentials with increase the pH of medium. The pH dependence of E pa can be expressed by the linear regression equation as: E pa(V)=−0.0567pH−0.2391 (R 2 =0.9974) (Fig. 4C), indicating that the proton is involved in the electrochemical reaction of DOX on PARG-GCE. The calculated slope of 0.0567V pH−1 is close to the theoretical value of 0.0589V pH−1which indicates that the number of electrons and protons in the electrochemical oxidation of DOX at the proposed electrode is equal according to Nernst equation.

The influence of scan rate on DOX oxidation at the PARG-GCE was analyzed by CV. Fig. 3 (B and C) shows the CVs of PARG-GCE in the absence (only PBS) and presence of 0.12M PBS solution (pH7.4) containing 25.86μM DOX at scan rates over the range of 2–1000mV/s. It is evident that there is no oxidation peak for PARG in the absence of DOX in different scan rates. On the other hand, the oxidation peak currents of PARG in the presence of DOX are enhanced as the scan rate increase. As illustrated in Fig. 5A the linear regression equation can be expressed as I pa(μA)=44.396 v (Vs−1)−13.55 (R 2 =0.9958). This behavior suggests that the oxidation process of DOX follows adsorptive-controlled mechanism at the PARG-GCE which was confirmed by a linear relation between I pa and square roots of scan rate (Fig. 5B). In addition, there was a linear relation between lnI pa and lnν, corresponding to the following equation: lnI pa(μA)=0.8547 lnν (Vs−1)−10.28 (R 2 =0.9984). The slope of 0.86 indicates that the electrode process is almost absolutely controlled by surface adsorption. These results were confirmed by relation between I pa and square roots of ν (Fig. 5B), corresponding to the following equation: lnI pa(μA)=0.8547 lnν (Vs−1)−10.28 (R 2 =0.9984).

According to the equations of the Laviron model, the electrochemical parameters of the electrode reaction can be estimated by exploring the relationship between the peak current of the redox couples and scan rates (nΔEp >200mV) [40]:
(1)
ln
k s
=
α s
ln
1 −
α s
+
1 −
α s
ln
α s
− ln
RT nFv
−
α s
1 −
α s
nF
∆ E
p
RT
where α is the charge transfer coefficient with a value of 0.5 and the remaining parameters have the usual meanings as explained in Eq. (1). The apparent heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k
s ) was calculated to be 10.1s−1. The high value of k
s indicates a faster electrode reaction due to the redox reaction at the PARG-GCE.

A plot of E vs. ln I (Fig. 5C), known as a Tafel plot, is a useful device for evaluating kinetic parameters. The Tafel slope was determined using the following equation for the anodic reactions [40]:
(2)
E = ln
I 0
+
RT nF
lnI
where I 0 and α are the exchange current and the transfer coefficient of the catalytic oxidation of DOX, respectively. The Tafel plot derived from the rising part of a voltammograms under quasistatic conditions for the oxidation of 25.86μM DOX at a scan rate of 20mV/s. A Tafel slope of 28.459V decade−1 is obtained indicating a charge transfer coefficient of 0.82 of the oxidation reaction. It can be seen that the value of α is quite close to its theoretical value. Furthermore, it can be noted that the oxidation peak potential (E pa) shifts positively with the increase of scan rate (Fig. 5D), which confirms the quasi-reversibility of the oxidation process. The linear regression equation of the E pa versus the logarithms of scan rates is expressed as: E pa(V)=0.0082 lnv (Vs−1)+0.679 (R 2 =0.9633). For an irreversible electrode process, Laviron equation can be expressed as [40]:
(3)
E p
=
RT
2 αf
lnv + constant
where R, F and T have their usual meanings, n is the number of electron transferred and α is the electron transfer coefficient. Thus the number of electrons (n) involved during DOX oxidation in PBS was calculated to be 1 at PARG-GCE. We found that for scan rates above 350mV/s the values of Epa were proportional to the logarithm of the scan rate.

The surface concentration of electroactive species, Γ
⁎ , can be estimated from the slope of the linear relation of peak current as a function of the scan rate, as represented by Eq. (4):
(4)
I p
=
n 2
F 2
4 RT
vA
Γ ∗
A
where n, A, Γ
⁎ and ν represent the number of electrons involved in the reaction, the electrode surface area, the surface coverage of the redox species (μmolcm−2) and the potential scan rate, respectively. From the slope of anodic peak currents vs. scan rate (Fig. 5(B)), the calculated surface concentration of DOX is 8.43×10−9 molcm−2 for n =2. In addition, the formal potential is almost independent of the potential scan rate for sweep rates below than 150mV/s suggesting facile charge transfer kinetics over this range of sweep rates (Fig. 6 ).

As a highly sensitive electrochemical method, the DPV has much higher current sensitivity and better resolution compared to CV. Therefore, DPV method is used for determination of DOX with the PARG-GCE. Under the optimum conditions, with the increase of DOX concentrations, the DPV oxidation peaks increased gradually in whole blood media (Fig. 7A). The results demonstrated that anodic peak current were proportional to one ranges of DOX concentration intervals (Fig. 7B), one is 1.03×10−7 to 3.45×10−6 M (whole blood) and 6.9×10−8 to 1.08×10−6 M (unprocessed plasma). The corresponding linear function of the prior for whole blood was I pa(μA)=4.18C (M)−2.7×10−7, with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.998.This range of detection and LLOQ are suitable for sensing DOX concentration in human body (3–900nM). Table 1 revealed the analytical performance of the proposed method in plasma and whole blood samples. A comparison of the analytical performances of the previously reported methods and the proposed method for the determination of DOX is summarized in Tables 2 and 3
. Although, in some cases the previous methods provide better limit of detection but it is important to mention that the reported methods were mostly utilized the plasma sample with the sophisticated and time-consuming sample preparation steps. However, sensing and biosensing in whole blood matrix were less reported.

FDA validation guidelines introduces the inter-day and intra-day evaluation methods to show the precision of the reported method where three concentrations (low, mid and high) of DOX were tested. For intra-day and inter-day precision, 5 replicated analyses of plasma and whole blood aliquots of DOX concentrations of 0.155, 0.345 and 0.862μM (for plasma) and 0.103, 0.862 and 2.56μM (for whole blood) were performed.

Tables 4 and 5
had shown the inter-day and intra-day precision, accuracy and recovery of the method in of plasma and whole blood samples. Concerning the maximum FDA permitted coefficient of variation value of 15%, the method has acceptable accuracy and precision.

These data revealed that the introduced method had an accepted level of precision. Recoveries varied from 89.2 to 103.7% by equating the calculated content with the nominal concentration of DOX. The experimental outcomes disclosed that the developed method was practical for the analysis of DOX in human whole blood and plasma samples.

The aim of drug stability testing procedures is to clear the ways that whether the drug is stable to environmental change including the matrix and storage conditions or not. Herein, we tested the short time temperature and freeze-thaw stability of the method to analysis of DOX in whole blood and plasma samples. As FDA recommended experimental procedure of short time temperature, we selected two different concentrations of DOX across the dynamic range and left the samples to stand at room temperature for at least 24h before analysis. Results showed that the differences between the values measured in fresh samples and the prepared short time temperature samples were in the FDA allowed range. These results reflect that the DOX aliquots were stable at room temperature for at least 24h. Also, freeze-thaw stability of the method were tested using whole blood and plasma aliquots containing DOX concentrations of 1.5×10−7 and 8.62×10−7 M (for plasma) 1.03×10−7 and 8.62×10−7 M (for whole blood) where the three FDA recommended freeze-thaw cycles were tested. As clear in Table 6 , the proposed analytical method possesses the allowed variations of lower 15% for whole plasma and whole blood samples.

The selectivity of the synthesized electrode over interfering agents were also tested, where the effect of vast range of coexisting substances over the DPV signal of the PARG-GCE electrode were checked in whole blood and plasma samples. Tables 7 and 8
list the possible interfering agents and their edge concentrations and also the current of the probe in presence and absence of the interfering agents. The interfering effect was calculated by the differences between the peak current in presence and absence of the interfering agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination in whole blood and plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of method to detect the DOX in whole blood and plasma samples were tested by preparing a series of spiked whole blood and plasma samples. The calculated values of the DOX are presented in Table 9 . Indeed, by utilizing these samples the power of the developed method to DOX analysis in presence of possible interfering matrix agents could be evaluated.

First, the A549 cells were treated with 20μM of DOX and protected in different incubation time (1, 2, 12, 24, and 48h).Then, the total protein of the each treated group was extracted. Finally, the PARG-GCE was exposed to the extracted total proteins at the optimum conditions. The DPVs were recorded and the results were presented in Fig. 8 (A & B). As can be seen, DOX was detected in different incubation times. These results show that application of engineered sensor for detection of DOX in cell lysates.

In this study, a rapid and sensitive method for the detection of DOX in some biological fluids is proposed. Using electro-synthesis of poly amino acid on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (PARG), the peak current responses of DOX were appeared in low potential (about −0.7V vs. Ag/AgCl) and therefore oxidation peak shifted toward less negative potential at the PARG-GCE surface. Also, electrooxidation peak currents were increased which the remarkable signal amplification can be attributed to the special properties of PARG. The preparation of this modified electrode is so easy and inexpensive. Moreover, the use of this electrode for electrochemical measurements of DOX has some advantages, such as fast electron transfer reaction, high stability, selectivity, repeatability and reproducibility as well as low limit of detection and wide linear range, and surface renewal. More importantly, this sensor was used for direct detection of DOX in real samples without necessary for extraction or pretreatments. Thereby this sensor has promising applications for the determination of trace amounts of DOX in biological fluids without complex pretreatments and interference from common species present in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by the Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center (PARC), Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.
